vulnerabilities.title: Copy-Paste Bug in decodeWellData: decimal1 Never Defaults to 18
vulnerabilities.summary: In Stable2.decodeWellData, the second conditional checks `decimal0 == 0` instead of `decimal1 == 0`. After the first block sets decimal0 to 18, the second block's condition (`decimal0 == 0`) can never be true. When well data encodes (0, 0) or (X, 0), decimal1 remains 0, causing getScaledReserves to multiply reserves[1] by 10^18 instead of the correct factor, corrupting ALL downstream LP supply, reserve, and rate calculations.

description.desc: Second if-block checks `decimal0 == 0` instead of `decimal1 == 0`; decimal1 never receives default value of 18

vulnerabilities.impact: Any Stable2 well deployed with decimal1=0 in its data encoding will have completely broken mathematics. The reserves[1] scaling error of 10^18 makes all LP supply, reserve, rate, and ratio calculations meaningless. This affects calcLpTokenSupply, calcReserve, calcRate, calcReserveAtRatioSwap, and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity. The pool would be immediately exploitable for total fund drainage through mispriced operations.

vulnerabilities.title: Silent Return of 0 on Non-Convergence in calcReserveAtRatioSwap/Liquidity
vulnerabilities.summary: Both calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity declare a named return variable `reserve` but never assign it outside the for loop. If Newton's method fails to converge within 255 iterations, the functions implicitly return 0 (Solidity default). Unlike calcReserve which explicitly reverts on non-convergence, these functions silently return an invalid value.

description.desc: For loop exits without return or revert; named return variable `reserve` defaults to 0; contrast with calcReserve line 124 which reverts

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. A return value of 0 from a reserve calculation is never valid for pools with non-zero reserves. If consumed by downstream protocol logic (oracle updates, swap calculations, liquidity operations), it could lead to: (1) Incorrect pricing enabling free or near-free token extraction; (2) Broken oracle values propagated to Beanstalk or other integrating protocols; (3) LP value destruction through incorrect invariant calculations. The contrast with calcReserve's explicit revert confirms this is an unintended omission.

vulnerabilities.title: _authorizeUpgrade Queries Aquifer Mapping in Wrong Direction, Permanently Bricking Upgrades
vulnerabilities.summary: _authorizeUpgrade calls `IAquifer(aquifer).wellImplementation(_getImplementation())` and `IAquifer(aquifer).wellImplementation(newImplmentation)`. The Aquifer mapping is `proxy_address → implementation_address`, but the code passes implementation addresses (not proxy addresses) as the lookup key. For ERC-1167 minimal proxies, `_getImplementation()` reads the ERC1967 slot which is never initialized (returns address(0)). Both require statements will always fail, permanently preventing any upgrade.

description.desc: wellImplementation(_getImplementation()) passes impl address to mapping that expects proxy address; ERC1967 slot never initialized for ERC-1167 clones

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. All WellUpgradeable instances bored through Aquifer are permanently non-upgradeable. This defeats the entire purpose of using UUPS upgradeability pattern. If a critical vulnerability is found in the Well logic, there is no on-chain remediation path - funds would need to be migrated to entirely new contracts. The bug is a combination of (1) misunderstanding the Aquifer mapping direction (proxy→impl vs impl→proxy) and (2) incompatibility between ERC-1167 minimal proxy pattern and ERC-1967 storage slots.

vulnerabilities.title: Missing Access Control on upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall Allows Unauthorized Upgrades
vulnerabilities.summary: WellUpgradeable.upgradeTo() and upgradeToAndCall() override OpenZeppelin's UUPS functions but do not include `onlyOwner` or any msg.sender check. The _authorizeUpgrade function only validates the new implementation against the Aquifer registry, not the caller's identity. If the _authorizeUpgrade checks were functional (currently broken per TECH-H003), any address could upgrade any WellUpgradeable proxy to any Aquifer-registered implementation.

description.desc: No onlyOwner modifier on upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall; _authorizeUpgrade has no msg.sender check; OwnableUpgradeable inherited but onlyOwner never applied to upgrade path

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL code defect. If TECH-H003 is independently fixed without also adding onlyOwner, any address can upgrade any WellUpgradeable proxy to any Aquifer-registered implementation. This would enable complete loss of all pool assets. The defect exists in the code regardless of current exploitability - the access control is simply missing from a function that requires it per the UUPS pattern specification.

vulnerabilities.title: calcLpTokenSupply divides by zero when one reserve is zero (but not both), potentially blocking operations in depleted-reserve states
vulnerabilities.summary: calcLpTokenSupply only short-circuits when both reserves are zero. If one reserve is zero and the other is non-zero, the function proceeds and computes dP using division by (scaledReserves[x] * N), which will be zero and revert. Any state-changing path that calls calcLpTokenSupply (directly or via calcRate/calcReserveAtRatioSwap) can be forced to revert when the pool becomes one-sided.

description.desc: only checks (r0==0 && r1==0); then dP = dP*D/(scaledReserves[0]*N) and /(scaledReserves[1]*N) => div by zero if either reserve is 0
Affected function: calcRate(uint256[],uint256,uint256,bytes)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - When one reserve is zero, all mathematical functions in Stable2 that depend on calcLpTokenSupply revert. This includes rate calculations used by oracles/pumps, reserve-at-ratio calculations used by external protocols (Beanstalk), and potentially swap/withdrawal operations in Well.sol. The developer's comment acknowledges this but claims 'only withdrawal will work,' which may be incorrect if withdrawal logic requires LP supply calculation. This creates a denial-of-service condition for the entire pool.

vulnerabilities.title: Reserve update step can underflow/overflow and revert during Newton iterations, allowing attackers to force deterministic DoS for specific target prices
vulnerabilities.summary: updateReserve adjusts `reserve` by a step proportional to |target-current|/(high-low). If currentPrice drifts outside the LUT bracket or deviates more than the bracket width, the ratio can exceed 1 and the step can exceed maxStepSize and even the reserve itself. The subtraction path will then underflow and revert. Because calcReserveAtRatioSwap/Liquidity loop calls updateReserve repeatedly, an attacker can search for inputs that reliably make the solver revert, blocking any state-changing operat

description.desc: reserve - step or reserve + step, where step = maxStepSize * |target-current| / (high-low); no clamp; subtraction can underflow
Affected function: updateReserve(PriceData,uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact: DoS for calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity for specific reserve/ratio combinations. If these functions are used in state-changing Well operations (swaps, liquidity adds/removes, oracle updates), the DoS can block critical protocol functionality. The impact is HIGH for availability. While financial loss is indirect (blocking withdrawals, forcing users to alternative routes), the code defect is clear: the missing clamp means the mathematical solver can revert instead of converging or gracefully handling divergence.

vulnerabilities.title: calcLpTokenSupply Returns Inaccurate Value on Non-Convergence Without Reverting
vulnerabilities.summary: calcLpTokenSupply uses Newton's method with 255 max iterations and a convergence tolerance of 1. If the method fails to converge (e.g., with extremely imbalanced reserves), the function returns the last computed lpTokenSupply value without reverting. Unlike the named return variable in calcReserveAtRatio functions (which defaults to 0), this returns a potentially inaccurate but non-zero value. This imprecise D value propagates to calcReserveAtRatioSwap (which uses it for parityReserve and initia

description.desc: Named return `lpTokenSupply` retains last iteration value on loop exhaustion with no revert; contrast with calcReserve which has explicit `revert("did not find convergence")`

vulnerabilities.impact: The missing revert is a confirmed code defect. If the Newton loop fails to converge, the function returns an imprecise D value that propagates to calcReserveAtRatioSwap (used for oracle/pricing), calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity, and calcRate. While practical exploitation requires crafting inputs that prevent convergence in 255 iterations (which is hard but not provably impossible for the StableSwap formula with A=1), the defect represents a safety violation. The impact is potentially high: incorrect D values could lead to mispriced swaps, incorrect oracle readings, and value extraction from pools.

vulnerabilities.title: WellUpgradeable init() Lacks Access Control Allowing Ownership Hijack via Re-initialization
vulnerabilities.summary: The init() function in WellUpgradeable uses reinitializer(2) but has no access control on who can call it. If a WellUpgradeable proxy was initially set up via initNoWellToken() (which uses initializer, setting initialized=1), then anyone can call init() since reinitializer(2) only requires that the initialized version is < 2. The init() function calls __Ownable_init() which sets msg.sender as the owner. An attacker calling init() becomes the owner of the Well, gaining control over any owner-prot

description.desc: init() is external with reinitializer(2) but no access control; __Ownable_init() sets msg.sender as owner; initNoWellToken() sets version to 1, leaving init() callable

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - An attacker can become the owner of any WellUpgradeable proxy that was initialized with initNoWellToken(). While the current _authorizeUpgrade implementation is broken for other reasons, ownership provides: (1) control via OwnableUpgradeable functions (transferOwnership, renounceOwnership), (2) potential future control if onlyOwner is added to upgrade functions, (3) any owner-gated functionality inherited from Well or added in future upgrades. The code defect exists regardless of current exploit profitability.

vulnerabilities.title: Downward Truncation in calcReserve Enables Value Extraction
vulnerabilities.summary: The `calcReserve` function truncates the required reserve downwards due to floor division when scaling back to the token's native decimals. This causes the pool to under-calculate the required reserve, leaving excess tokens in the pool which are then sent to the swapper. An attacker can repeatedly swap to extract this rounding error, draining the pool.

description.desc: Floor division `reserve / (10 ** (18 - decimals[j]))` truncates downward

vulnerabilities.impact: NEGLIGIBLE practical impact. The code defect is real (wrong rounding direction), but the maximum value leakage is less than 1 unit of the token's smallest denomination per operation. For USDC: <$0.000001 per swap. This does not constitute a meaningful economic risk to LPs. The hypothesis's CRITICAL rating and 'drain the pool' claim are vastly overstated. A more accurate severity would be INFORMATIONAL or LOW.

vulnerabilities.title: Permanent DoS of Well via Underflow when Amplification Parameter A < 25
vulnerabilities.summary: `Stable2` reads the amplification parameter `a` from the provided LUT contract. In `calcLpTokenSupply`, the denominator includes `(Ann - A_PRECISION)`. Since `Ann = a * 4` and `A_PRECISION = 100`, if `a < 25`, `Ann` will be less than 100, causing `Ann - A_PRECISION` to underflow and revert. Any pool using a `Stable2` instance configured with a LUT returning `a < 25` will be permanently unusable.

description.desc: Underflow in `(Ann - A_PRECISION)` if `Ann < 100`
Affected function: getAParameter()

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect is a missing input validation in the constructor that can cause permanent DoS of any pool using the misconfigured Stable2 instance. While the default Stable2LUT1 returns A=100 (safe), the permissionless nature of the system means anyone could deploy a Stable2 with a bad LUT. The impact is permanent pool bricking - not fund loss, but complete functional denial. All core functions (calcLpTokenSupply, calcReserve, calcRate, calcReserveAtRatioSwap, calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity) become unreachable.

vulnerabilities.title: Silent 0 return on non-convergence in reserve-at-ratio functions can be weaponized for incorrect quotes and fund loss
vulnerabilities.summary: Stable2.calcReserveAtRatioSwap and Stable2.calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity do not revert or return a safe value when the 255-iteration Newton loop fails to meet the PRICE_THRESHOLD condition. Because they have named return variables and no terminal revert/return, they can silently return 0. If a Well or integration uses this reserve value to compute required inputs/outputs, a 0 reserve quote can cause swaps/mints/burns to execute at extreme mispricing.

description.desc: named return variable `reserve` with no assignment on non-convergence -> implicit return 0
Affected function: calcRate(uint256[],uint256,uint256,bytes)

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect is confirmed: both calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity can silently return 0 on non-convergence. The severity depends on how downstream code (Well contract, Beanstalk protocol) handles the 0 return value. If treated as valid, this could enable zero-cost swaps, incorrect oracle readings, or pool drainage. Even without a confirmed downstream exploit path (Well code not provided), the defect itself is critical because: (1) it's inconsistent with calcReserve's explicit revert pattern, (2) a 0 reserve return is never a valid result for a functioning pool, and (3) it silently fails rather than alerting callers to the computation failure.

vulnerabilities.title: LUT Price Range Limitation Causes DoS at Extreme Reserve Ratios
vulnerabilities.summary: Stable2LUT1 reverts for prices outside [0.001083e6, 10.37089e6] (liquidity) and [0.001083e6, 10.709509e6] (swap). If pool reserves are manipulated to create a target price outside these ranges, calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity will revert. This creates a DoS condition for any protocol operation depending on these functions, including oracle updates and liquidation calculations.

description.desc: revert('LUT: Invalid price') for price < 0.001083e6 or price > 10.37089e6/10.709509e6
Affected function: calcReserveAtRatioSwap

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The DoS affects calcReserveAtRatioSwap and calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity, which are used by external protocols (e.g., Beanstalk's Multi Flow Pump) for oracle price calculations and reserve targeting. When blocked: (1) Oracle updates fail, potentially staling price feeds; (2) Liquidation calculations fail, potentially preventing critical protocol safety operations; (3) Any integration using IMultiFlowPumpWellFunction or IBeanstalkWellFunction interfaces is affected. Recovery is possible by rebalancing the pool, but during the blocked period, the protocol operates without these critical functions.

vulnerabilities.title: calcRate can revert via underflow if computed reserve increases, causing DoS in any path that depends on rate (including ratio solvers)
vulnerabilities.summary: Stable2._calcRate computes `rate = _reserves[i] - calcReserve(...)`. If `calcReserve` returns a value >= original `_reserves[i]`, the subtraction underflows and reverts (Solidity 0.8+). For extreme reserve states (very large reserves where adding 1e6 is negligible, or numerical instability), calcReserve may not decrease as expected, making rate computation brittle. Any operation that calls calcRate (directly or via calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity loop) could be forced to revert.

description.desc: rate = _reserves[i] - calcReserve(...); subtraction can underflow/revert if calcReserve >= _reserves[i]
Affected function: calcRate(uint256[],uint256,uint256,bytes)

vulnerabilities.title: `proxiableUUID` Modifier Logic Error Bricks Upgrades
vulnerabilities.summary: The `notDelegatedOrIsMinimalProxy` modifier on `proxiableUUID` reverts on direct calls (`address(this) == ___self`). During an upgrade, OpenZeppelin's `_upgradeToAndCallUUPS` calls `proxiableUUID` directly on the *new* implementation contract to verify it. Because it is a direct call, the modifier hits the `else` block and reverts, permanently bricking all upgrades.

description.desc: else { revert("UUPSUpgradeable: must not be called through delegatecall"); }

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH — All WellUpgradeable proxies are permanently unable to upgrade their implementations. This means any bugs, vulnerabilities, or required improvements can never be patched through the upgrade mechanism. Combined with the missing onlyOwner check on _authorizeUpgrade (which would be moot since upgrades revert anyway), the entire upgradeability feature is non-functional. If a critical vulnerability is discovered in the Well logic, there is no way to remediate it via upgrade.
